Cloud Applications
10.17
<!-- image -->
Cloud computing is a rapidly moving target. New technological advances and application services are regularly introduced. There exist many open challenges especially in the context of energy-effi cient management of Data Centers and marketplace for Cloud computing. This chapter presents an overview of various open issues in Cloud computing that need long-term investigation. It discusses issues involved in energy effi cient Cloud computing and presents a Green
cloud computing architecture. It discusses market models needed for realizing an open market for Cloud computing systems from the perspective of federations of Clouds and agreements between Clouds. A general overview of some of the existing standards that enable interoperation between Clouds and a brief look at third party Cloud services is presented. 11.1 ENERGY EFFICIENCY IN CLOUDS
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Modern data centers, operating under the Cloud computing model are hosting a variety of applications
This is the content -  ranging from those that run for a few seconds (e.g., serving requests of Web applications such as ecommerce and social networks portals) to those that run for longer periods of time (e.g., simulations or large dataset processing) on shared hardware platforms. The need to manage multiple applications in a data center creates the challenge of on-demand resource provisioning and allocation in response to time-varying workloads. Normally, data center resources are statically allocated to applications, based on peak load characteristics, in order to maintain isolation and provide performance guarantees. Until recently, high performance has been the sole concern in data center deployments and this demand has been fulfi lled without paying much attention to energy consumption. According to McKinsey report on "Revolutionizing Data Center Energy Effi ciency" [118], a typical data center consumes as much energy as 25,000 households. The total energy bill for data centers in 2010 was over $11 billion, and energy costs in a typical data center doubles every fi ve years. As energy costs are increasing while availability dwindles, there is a need to shift focus from optimising data center resource management for pure performance alone to optimising for energy effi ciency while maintaining high service level performance (see Fig. 11.1).
11.2
Mastering Cloud Computing
User Applications
<!-- image -->
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Modern data centers, operating under the Cloud computing model are hosting a variety of applications
This is the content -  Data centers are not only expensive to maintain, but also unfriendly to the environment. Carbon emis- sion due to data centers worldwide is now more than both Argentina and the Netherlands [118] emission. High energy costs and huge carbon footprints are incurred due to the massive amount of electricity needed to power and cool the numerous servers hosted in these data centers. Cloud service providers need to adopt measures to ensure that their profi t margin is not dramatically reduced due to high energy costs. According to Amazon.com's estimate, the energy-related costs of its data centers amount to 42% of the total budget that include both direct power consumption and the cooling infrastructure amortized over a 15-year period. As a result, companies such as Google, Microsoft, and Yahoo are building large data centers in barren desert land surrounding the Columbia River, USA to exploit cheap hydroelectric power. There is also increasing pressure from Governments worldwide to reduce carbon footprints, which have a signifi cant impact on climate change. To address these concerns, leading IT vendors have recently formed a global consortium, called The Green Grid, to promote energy effi ciency for data centers and minimize their impact on the environmental. Pike Research forecasts that data center energy expenditures will reduce from $23.3 billion in 2010 to $16.0 billion in 2020, as well as causing a 28 percent reduction in GHG (green house gas) emissions from 2010 levels as a result of adoption of Cloud computing model in delivering IT services. Lowering the energy usage of data centers is a challenging and complex issue because computing applications and data are growing so quickly that larger servers and disks are needed to process them
fast enough within the required time period. Green Cloud computing is envisioned to achieve not only effi cient processing and utilisation of computing infrastructure, but also to minimise energy consumption. This is essential for ensuring that the future growth of Cloud computing is sustainable. Cloud computing, with increasingly pervasive front-end client devices, such as iPhones interacting with backend data centers, will cause an enormous escalation of energy usage. To address this problem, data center resources need to be managed in an energy-effi cient manner to drive Green Cloud computing.
Advanced Topics in Cloud Computing
<!-- image -->
11.3
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Modern data centers, operating under the Cloud computing model are hosting a variety of applications
This is the content -  In particular, Cloud resources need to be allocated not only to satisfy QoS requirements specifi ed by users via Service Level Agreements (SLAs), but also to reduce energy usage. This can be achieved by applying market-based utility models to accept user requests that can be fulfi lled to enhance revenue along with energy-effi cient utilisation of Cloud infrastructure. 11.1.1 Energy-Effi cient and Green
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Cloud-Computing Architecture A high-level architecture for supporting energy-effi cient resource allocation in Green Cloud computing infrastructure is shown in Fig. 11.2. It consists of four main components:
This is the content -  Consumers
(or their
<!-- image -->
Cloud IaaS
(a) Consumers/Brokers. Cloud consumers or their brokers submit service requests from
anywhere in the world to the Cloud. It is important to notice that there can be a difference between Cloud consumers and users of deployed services. For instance, a consumer can be a company deploying a Web application, which presents varying workload according to the number of "users" accessing it. (b) Green Resource Allocator. Acts as the interface between the Cloud infrastructure and consumers. It requires the interaction of the following components to support energy-effi cient resource management: Green Negotiator. Negotiates with the consumers/brokers to fi nalize the SLAs with specifi ed
prices and penalties (for violations of SLAs) between the Cloud provider and consumer depending on the consumer's QoS requirements and energy saving schemes. In case of Web applications, for instance, QoS metric can be 95% of requests being served in less than 3 seconds. Service Analyzer. Interprets and analyzes the service requirements of a submitted request be- fore deciding whether to accept or reject it. Hence, it needs the latest load and energy information from VM Manager and Energy Monitor respectively.
11.4
Mastering Cloud Computing
Consumer Profi ler. Gathers specifi c characteristics of consumers so that important consumers can be granted special privileges and prioritised over other consumers. Pricing. Decides how service requests are charged to manage the supply and demand of computing
resources and facilitate in prioritising service allocations effectively. Energy Monitor. Observes and determines which physical machines to power on/off.
Service Scheduler. Assigns requests to VMs and determines resource entitlements for allo- cated VMs. It also decides when VMs are to be added or removed to meet demand. VM Manager. Keeps track of the availability of VMs and their resource entitlements. It is also in
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Cloud-Computing Architecture A high-level architecture for supporting energy-effi cient resource allocation in Green Cloud computing infrastructure is shown in Fig. 11.2. It consists of four main components:
This is the content -  charge of migrating VMs across physical machines. Accounting. Maintains the actual usage of resources by requests to compute usage costs. His- torical usage information can also be used to improve service allocation decisions. (c) VMs. Multiple VMs can be dynamically started and stopped on a single physical machine to
meet accepted requests, hence providing maximum fl exibility to confi gure various partitions of resources on the same physical machine to different specifi c requirements of service requests. Multiple VMs can also concurrently run applications based on different operating system environments on a single physical machine. In addition, by dynamically migrating VMs across physical machines, workloads can be consolidated and unused resources can be put on a low-power state, turned off or confi gured to operate at low-performance levels (e.g., using DVFS—Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling) in order to save energy. (d) Physical Machines. The underlying physical computing servers provide hardware infra- structure for creating virtualized resources to meet service demands. 1. Energy-aware Dynamic Resource Allocation
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Recent developments in virtualisation have resulted in its usage across data centers. Virtualization enables dynamic migration of VMs across physical nodes according to QoS requirements. Unused VMs
This is the content -  can be logically resized and consolidated on a minimal number of physical nodes, while idle nodes can be turned off (or hibernated). Through consolidation of VMs, large number of users can share a single physical server, which increases utilization, and in turn, reduces the total number of servers required. Moreover, VM consolidation can be applied dynamically by capturing the workload variability and adapting the VM placement at run-time using migration. Currently, resource allocation in a Cloud data center aims at providing high performance while meeting SLAs, with limited or no consideration for energy consumption during VM allocations. However, to explore both performance and energy effi ciency, two crucial issues must be addressed. First, turning off resources in a dynamic environment puts QoS at risk; aggressive consolidation may cause some VMs to obtain insuffi cient resources to serve a spike in load. Second, agreed SLAs bring challenges to application performance management in virtualized environments. These issues require effective consolidation policies that can minimize energy use without compromising the user QoS requirements. The current approaches to dynamic VM consolidation are weak in terms of providing performance guarantees. One of the ways to prove performance bounds is to divide the problem of energy-effi cient dynamic VM consolidation into a few sub-problems that can be analyzed individually. It is important to analytically model the problem, and derive optimal and near optimal approximation algorithms that provide provable effi ciency. To achieve this goal, Clouds need novel analytical models and QoS-based resource allocation algorithms that optimise VM placements with the objective of minimizing energy consumption under performance constraints.
Advanced Topics in Cloud Computing
11.5
2. InterClouds and Integrated Allocation of Resources
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Cloud providers have been deploying data centers in multiple locations throughout the globe. For example, Amazon EC2 Cloud services are available via their data centers located in US, Europe, and Sin-
This is the content -  gapore. This is leading to the emergence of a notion, called "InterCloud", supporting scalable delivery of application services by harnessing multiple data centers from one or more providers. In addition to enhancing performance and reliability, these InterClouds provide a powerful means of reducing energyrelated costs. One reason is that the local demand for electricity varies with time of day, and weather. This causes time-varying differences in the price of electricity at each location. Moreover, each site has a different source of energy (such as coal, hydro-electric, wind), with different environmental costs. This gives scope to adjust the load sent to each location, and the number of servers powered on at each location, to improve effi ciency. In such environment, algorithms that make routing decisions by considering the location of the user, the energy-effi ciency of the hardware at each site, the energy mix, and the number of servers currently on at each location are needed. A particularly promising approach is to use this routing to make work "follow the renewables". A major problem with renewable energy is that most sources are intermittent and uncontrollable. Dynamically routing requests to locations with available renewable energy can greatly reduce the non-renewable energy used and facilitate the widespread use of clean energy. Sending load to remote data centers incurs both delay costs and energy costs, due to the increased amount of data that is transferred over the Internet. Improvements in the energy effi cient transport tech-
nology should lead to signifi cant reductions in the power consumption of the Cloud software services [120]. 11.2 MARKET-BASED MANAGEMENT OF CLOUDS
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Cloud computing is still in infancy and its prominent use is twofold: i) complete replacement of in-house
This is the content -  IT infrastructure and services with the same capabilities rented by service providers; ii) elastic scaling of existing computing systems in order to address peak workloads. The efforts in research and industry have been mostly oriented to design and implement systems that actually enable business vendors and enterprises to achieve these goals. The real potential of Cloud computing resides in the fact that it actually facilitates the establishment of a market for trading IT utilities. This opportunity until now has been mildly explored and falls in the domain of what is called Market-Oriented Cloud computing [30]. 11.2.1 Market-Oriented Cloud Computing
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Cloud computing already embodies the concept of providing IT assets as utilities. Then, what makes Cloud computing different from Market-Oriented Cloud computing? First, it is important to understand
This is the content -  what do we intend with the term "market". The Oxford English Dictionary (OED) 60 defi nes market as a " place where a trade is conducted" (Def. I). More precisely, it refers to a meeting or a gathering together of people for the purchase and sale of goods. A broader characterization, defi nes the term "market" as the action of buying and selling, to a commercial transaction, a purchase, or a bargain. Therefore, essential is the act of trading mostly performed in an environment—either physical or virtual—that is specifi cally dedicated to such activity. If we consider how IT assets and services are consumed as utilities, it is evident that there is a trade between the service provider and the consumer; this enables the use of the service by the user under a given Service Level Agreement (SLA). Therefore, Cloud computing already expresses the concept of trade, even though the interaction between consumer and provider is not as sophisticated as happens in real markets: users generally select one Cloud computing vendor among a group of competing providers
60
The defi nition of market according to the OED can be found at: http://www.oed.com/view/Entry/114178? rskey=13s2aI&amp;
result=1#eid
.
[Retrieved: 07/05/2011].
11.6
Mastering Cloud Computing and leverage its services as long as they need them. Moreover, at present, most service providers
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Cloud computing already embodies the concept of providing IT assets as utilities. Then, what makes Cloud computing different from Market-Oriented Cloud computing? First, it is important to understand
This is the content -  have infl exible pricing, generally limited to fl at rates or tariffs based on usage thresholds. Also, many providers have proprietary interfaces to their services, thus restricting the ability of consumers to quickly move—and with minimal conversion costs—between one vendor to another. This rigidity is known as vendor lock-in and undermines the potential of Cloud computing to be an open market where services are freely traded. Therefore, in order to remove such restrictions, it is required that vendors expose services through standard interfaces. This enables full commoditization and thus, would pave the way for the creation of a market infrastructure for trading services. What differentiates Market-Oriented Cloud Computing (MOCC) is the presence of a virtual market place where IT services are traded and brokered dynamically. This is something that still has to be achieved, and that will signifi cantly evolve the way in which Cloud computing services are eventually delivered to the consumer. More precisely, what is missing is the availability of a market where desired services are published and then automatically bid by matching the requirements of customers and providers. At present, some Cloud computing vendors are already moving in this direction 61 : This phenomenon is happening in the Infrastructure as a Service domain—which is the market sector that is more consolidated and mature for Cloud computing—but it has not taken off broadly yet. We can clearly characterize the relation between Cloud computing and MOCC as follows: "Market Oriented Computing has the same characteristics as Cloud Computing; therefore it is a dy-
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Cloud computing already embodies the concept of providing IT assets as utilities. Then, what makes Cloud computing different from Market-Oriented Cloud computing? First, it is important to understand
This is the content -  namically provisioned unifi ed computing resource allowing you to manage software and data storage as on aggregate capacity resulting in "real-time" infrastructure across public and private infrastructures. Market Oriented Cloud Computing goes one step further by allowing spread into multiple public and hybrid environments dynamically composed by trading service." [122] The realization of this vision is technically possible today, but not probable given the lack of standards and overall immaturity of the market. Nonetheless, it is expected that in the near future with the introduction of standards, concerns on security and trust will begin to disappear and enterprises will feel more comfortable in leveraging a market-oriented model for integrating IT infrastructure and services from the Cloud. Moreover, the presence of a demand-based market place represents an opportunity for enterprises to shape their infrastructure for dynamically reacting to workload spikes and cut maintenance costs. It also allows possibility to temporary lease some in-house capacity during low usage periods, thus providing a better Return on Investment. This will lead to the complete realization of Market-Oriented Cloud computing. 11.2.2 A Reference Model for MOCC
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Market-Oriented Cloud computing originates from the coordination of several components: service con-
This is the content -  sumers, service providers, and other entities that make the trading between these two groups possible. Market-orientation does not only infl uence the organization at global scale of the Cloud computing market. It also shapes the internal architecture of Cloud computing providers needing to support a more fl exible allocation of their resources, which is driven by additional parameters such as those defi ning the quality of service. 1. A Global View of Market-Oriented Cloud Computing
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  A reference scenario that realizes Market-Oriented Cloud computing at a global scale is given in Fig. 11.3. It provides guidance on how MOCC can be implemented in practice.
This is the content -  Several component and entities contribute to the defi nition of a global market-oriented architecture. The fundamental component is the virtual market place—represented by the Cloud Exchange (CEx)—
which acts as a market maker for bringing service producers and consumers together. The principal
61
Amazon introduced the concept of spot instances that are dynamically offered by the provider according to their availability and bid by the customer. Their effective usage and consumption is then determined by the spot price established by Amazon and the
maximum price provided by the customers.
Advanced Topics in Cloud Computing
11.7
players in the virtual marketplace are the Cloud Coordinators and the Cloud Brokers. The fi rst ones represent the Cloud vendors and publish the services that are offered by them, the others—operating on behalf of the consumers—identify the subset of services that are matching the customers' requirement in terms of service profi le and quality of service. Brokers perform the same function as they would do in the real world: they mediate between coordinators and consumers by acquiring services from the fi rst ones and sub-leasing them to second ones. They can accept requests from many users. At the same time, users can leverage different brokers. A similar relationship can be considered between coordinators and Cloud computing services vendors. Coordinators take the responsibility of publishing and advertising services on behalf of the vendors and can gain benefi ts from reselling them to brokers. Every single participant has his own utility function that they want to optimize. Negotiations and trades are carried out in a secure and dependable environment and are mostly driven by SLAs, which each party has to fulfi ll. There might be different models for negotiations among entities, even though the auction model seems to be the more appropriate in the current scenario. The same consideration can be made for the pricing models: price can be fi xed but it is expected that they will most likely change according to the market conditions.
VMM
<!-- image -->
Pool Node
Several components contribute to the realization of the Cloud Exchange and implement its features. In the reference model depicted in the fi gure, it is possible to identify three major components: (a) Directory. The market directory contains a listing of all the published services that are avail-
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  A reference scenario that realizes Market-Oriented Cloud computing at a global scale is given in Fig. 11.3. It provides guidance on how MOCC can be implemented in practice.
This is the content -  able in the Cloud marketplace. The directory does not only contain a simple mapping between service names and the corresponding vendor (or Cloud coordinators) offering them. It also provides additional metadata that can help the brokers or the end users in fi ltering among the services of interest those that
11.8
Mastering Cloud Computing can really meet the expected quality of service. Moreover, several indexing methods can be provided in
order to optimize the discovery of services according to different criteria. This component is modifi ed in its content by service providers and queried by service consumers. (b) Auctioneer. The auctioneer is in charge of keeping track of the running auctions in the market place and of verifying that the auctions for services are properly conducted and that malicious market players are prevented from performing illegal activities. (c) Bank. The bank is the component that takes care of the fi nancial aspect of all the operations
happening in the virtual marketplace. It also ensures that all the fi nancial transactions are carried out in a secure and dependable environment. Consumers and providers may register with the bank and have one or multiple accounts that can be used to perform the transactions in the virtual marketplace. The organization described above constitutes only a reference model that is used to guide system archi- tects and designers in laying out the foundations of a Cloud Exchange system. In reality, the architecture of such a system is more complex and articulated since other elements have to be taken into account. For instance, since the Cloud marketplace supports trading, which ultimately involves fi nancial transactions between different parties, security becomes of fundamental importance. It is then important to put in place all the mechanisms that enable secure electronic transactions. These and other aspects are not unique to the design and implementation of MOCC systems but are of concern for any distributed computing system, therefore they have been only mentioned in this presentation. 2. Market-Oriented Architecture for Datacenters
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Datacenters are the building blocks of the computing infrastructure backing the services offered by a
This is the content -  Cloud computing vendor, despite its specifi c category (IaaS, PaaS, or SaaS). In this section, we present these systems by taking into account the elements that are fundamental for realizing computing infrastructures supporting Market-Oriented Cloud computing. These criteria govern the logical organization of these systems—rather than their physical layout and the hardware characteristics—and provide guidance for designing architectures that are market-oriented. In other words, we describe a reference architecture for Market-Oriented Cloud datacenters. Fig. 11.4 provides an overall view of the components that can support a Cloud computing provider in making available its services on a market-oriented basis [123]. More specifi cally, the model applies to Plat- form-as-a-Service and Infrastructure-as-a-Service providers, who explicitly leverage virtualization technologies to serve customers' needs. There are four major components that compose the architecture: (a) Users and Brokers. They originate the workload that is managed in the Cloud datacenter.
Users either require virtual machine instances where to deploy their systems (IaaS scenario) or deploy applications in the virtual environment made available to them by the provider (PaaS scenario). These service requests issued by service brokers that act on behalf of the users and look for the best deal for them. (b) SLA Resource Allocator. This represents the interface between the datacenter and the Cloud service provider and the external world. Its main responsibility is ensuring that service requests are satisfi ed according to the service level agreement agreed with the user. Several components coordinate their activities in order to realize this goal: Service Request Examiner and Admission Control Module. This module op-
erates in the front end, and fi lters the requests of users and brokers in order to accept those that are feasible, given the current status of the system and the workload it is already processing. Accepted requests are allocated and scheduled for execution. IaaS service providers allocate one or more virtual machine instances and make them available to the user. PaaS providers identify a suitable collection of computing nodes where to deploy the application of the user.
Advanced Topics in Cloud Computing
11.9
Pricing Module. This module is responsible for charging users according to the service level

<!-- image -->
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Datacenters are the building blocks of the computing infrastructure backing the services offered by a
This is the content -  agreement signed with them. Different parameters can be considered while charging users, for instance, the most common case for IaaS providers is charging according to the characteristics of virtual machine requested in term of memory, disk size, computing capacity and the time they are used. It is very common to discretize the usage in time blocks of one hour, but several other pricing schemes exist. In case of PaaS providers, users can be charged in terms of the number of requests served by their application or the usage of internal services made available by the development platform to the application while running. Accounting Module. This module maintains the actual usage of resources and stores the billing information for each user. These data are made available to the Service Request Examiner and Admission Control module when assessing users' requests. In addition, they constitute a rich source of information that can be mined to identify usage trends and improve the vendor's service offering. Dispatcher. This component is responsible for the low-level operations that are required to real-
ize admitted service requests. In an IaaS scenario, this module instructs the infrastructure to deploy as many virtual machines as they are needed to satisfy the user's request. In a PaaS scenario, this module
11.10
Mastering Cloud Computing activates and deploys the user's application on a selected set of nodes, deployment can happen either
within a virtual machine instance or within an appropriate sandboxed environment. Resource Monitor. This component monitors the status of the computing resources, either physical or virtual. Infrastructure-as-a-Service providers mostly focus on keeping track of the availability of VMs and their resource entitlements. Platform-as-a-Service providers monitor the status of the distributed middleware enabling the elastic execution of applications and load of each node. Service Request Monitor. This component keeps track of the execution progress of service
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Datacenters are the building blocks of the computing infrastructure backing the services offered by a
This is the content -  requests. The information collected through the service request monitor is helpful to analyze the performance of the system and to provide a quality feedback about the capability of the provider in satisfying requests. For instance, elements of interest are: the number of requests satisfi ed versus the number of incoming requests, the average processing time of a request, or its time-to-execution. This data is an important source of information for tuning the system. The SLA allocator executes the main logic that governs the operations of a single or a collection of datacenters. Features such as failure management are most likely to be addressed by other software module, which can either be a separate layer or can be integrated within the SLA resource allocator. (c) Virtual Machines (VMs). Virtual machines constitute the basic building blocks of a
Cloud computing infrastructure, especially for Infrastructure-as-a-Service providers. They represent the unit of deployment for addressing users' requests. Infrastructure management software is in charge of keeping operational the computing infrastructure backing the commercial service offering of the provider. As we already discussed, they play a fundamental role in providing an appropriate hosting environment for users' applications and—at the same time—isolate applications execution from the infrastructure, thus preventing applications from harming the hosting environment. Moreover, VMs are among the most important components infl uencing the quality of service which a user request is served with. VMs can be tuned in terms of their emulated hardware characteristics so that the amount of computing resource of the physical hardware allocated to a user can be fi nely controlled. Platform-as-a-Service providers do not directly expose virtual machine to the fi nal user, but may internally leverage virtualization technology in order to fully and securely utilize their own infrastructure. As previously discussed, PaaS providers often leverage a given middleware for executing users applications, and might use different QoS parameters to charge application execution, rather than the emulated hardware profi le. (d) Physical Machines. At the lowest level of the reference architecture resides the physi- cal infrastructure that can comprise one or more datacenters. This is the layer that provides the resources to meet service demands. This architecture provides Cloud services vendor with a reference model suitable for enabling their infra-
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Datacenters are the building blocks of the computing infrastructure backing the services offered by a
This is the content -  structure for market-oriented Cloud Computing. As mentioned before, these observations mostly apply to Platform-as-a-Service and Infrastructure-as-a-Service providers, while Software-as-a-Service vendors operate at a higher abstraction level. Despite this, it is still possible to identify some of the elements of the SLA Resource Allocator, which will be modifi ed in order to deal with the services offered by the provider. For instance, rather than linking end user requests to virtual machine instances and platform nodes, the allocator will be mostly concerned with scheduling the execution of this request within the SaaS framework of the provider, and lower layers in the technology stack will be in charge of controlling the computing infrastructure. Accounting, pricing, and service request monitoring will still perform their role. Regardless of the specifi c service offering category, the reference architecture discussed above aims to support Cloud computing vendors in delivering commercial solutions that are able to [123]:
● Support customer-driven service management based on customer profi les and requested service
- requirements. ● Defi ne computational risks management tactics to identify, assess, and manage risks involved in the execution of applications with regards to service requirements and customer needs.
●
Advanced Topics in Cloud Computing
11.11
Derive appropriate market-based resource management strategies encompassing both custom-
- er-driven service management and computational risk management in order to sustain SLAoriented resource allocation. ● Incorporate autonomic resource management models that effectively self-manage changes in service requirements to satisfy both new service demands and existing service obligation.
- Leverage—when appropriate—VM technology to dynamically assign resource shares according to service requirements.
- These capabilities are of fundamental importance for competitiveness of Cloud computing market and
for addressing a scenario characterized by dynamic SLA negotiations as it is envisioned by MOCC. Currently, there is no or limited support for such a dynamic negotiation model, which constitute the next step towards the full realization of Cloud computing. 11.2.3 Technologies and Initiatives
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Supporting MOCC Existing Cloud computing solutions have very limited support for market-oriented strategies for deliver-
This is the content -  ing services to customer. Most of the current solutions are mostly focused on enabling Cloud computing for concerns such as the delivery of infrastructure, distributed runtime environments, and services. Since Cloud computing has been recently adopted, the consolidation of the technology constitutes the fi rst step towards the full realization of its promises. Until now, a lot of interest has been directed towards Infrastructure-as-a-Service solutions, which represents a well consolidated sector in the Cloud computing market, with several different players and competitive offers. New Platform-as-a-Service solutions are gaining momentum and it is harder for them to penetrate in the market dominated by giants such as Google , Microsoft, and Force.com . 1. Framework for Trading Computing Utilities
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  From an academic point of view, a considerable amount of research has been carried out in defi ning
This is the content -  models enabling the trading of computing utilities, with a specifi c focus on the design of market-oriented schedulers for Grid computing systems. As discussed in the introduction, computing Grids aggregate a heterogeneous set of resources that are geographically distributed and might belong to different organizations. The use of such resources is often leased for long term usage by means of agreements among these organizations. Within this context, market-oriented schedulers—which are aware of the price of a given computing resource and schedule user's applications according to their budget—have been investigated and implemented. The research in this area is of relevance to market-oriented Cloud computing, since Cloud computing leverages the pre-existing distributed computing technologies including Grid Computing. Garg and Buyya [124] have provided a complete taxonomy and analysis of such schedulers, which is reported in Fig. 11.5. A major classifi cation categorizes these schedulers according to: allocation decision , objective , market-model , application-model, and participant focus. Of a particular interest is the classifi cation according to the market-model, which is the mechanism used for trading between users and providers. Along this dimension, it is possible to classify the schedulers into the following categories: (a) Game Theory. In market models that are based on game theory, participants interact in
the form of an allocation game with different payoffs as a result of specifi c actions employing various strategies. (b) Proportional Share. This market model originates from proportional share scheduling, which aims to allocate jobs fairly over a set of resources. This original concept has been contextualized within a market-oriented scenario where the shares of the cluster are directly proportional to the user's bid.
11.12
Mastering Cloud Computing
Market-Oriented Models
<!-- image -->
System
Centric
Other e.g., Storage
(c) Commodity Market. In this model, the resource provider specifi es the price of resourc-
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  From an academic point of view, a considerable amount of research has been carried out in defi ning
This is the content -  - es and charges users according to the amount of resource they consume. The determination of the price by the provider is the result of a decisional process involving investment and management costs, current demand and supply. Moreover, they might be subject to vary over time. (d) Posted Price. This model is similar to the commodity market but provider may make special
- offers and discounts to new clients. Also, with respect to the commodity market, prices are fi xed over time. (e) Contract-Net. In market-models based on the Contract-Net [125] protocol, users advertise
- their demand and invite resource owners to submit bids. Resource owners check these advertisements with respect to their requirements. If the advertisement is favorable to them, the providers will respond with a bid. The user, then, consolidates all the bids and compares them to select those most favorable to him. The providers are then informed about the outcome of their bid, which can be acceptance or rejection. (f) Bargaining. In market-models based on bargaining, the negotiation among resource con-
- sumers and providers is carried out until a mutual agreement is reached, or it is stopped when either of the parties is no more interested. (g) Auction. In market-models based on auctions, the price of resources is unknown and com-
- petitive bids regulated by a third party—the auctioneer—contribute to determine the fi nal price of a resource. The bid that ultimately sets the price of a resource is the winning bid and the corresponding user gains access to the resource. The most popular and interesting market model for trading computing utilities are the commodity market ,
posted price, and auction models. Commodity market and posted price, or variations/combinations of them, are driving the majority of the Cloud computing services offering. Auction-based models, instead, can potentially constitute the reference market-models for MOCC, since they are able to seamlessly support dynamic negotiations.
Advanced Topics in Cloud Computing
11.13
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  From an academic point of view, a considerable amount of research has been carried out in defi ning
This is the content -  Academic research has led to the development of a considerable number of software projects that implement either user resource brokers or resource management systems able to trade computing utilities according to one, or some of the market models described previously. Some of them—namely SHARP [126], Tycoon [127], Bellagio [128], and Shirako [129]—have focused on trading VM-based resource slices and are more likely to be applicable within market-oriented Cloud computing scenario. Also it is worth noting some relevant research projects focusing on resource brokering such as Nimrod-G [164] and Gridbus Broker [15], which have evolved to integrate capabilities for leasing Cloud computing resources. 2. Industrial Implementations
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Even though market-oriented models have been mostly developed in the academic domain, industrial
This is the content -  implementations of some aspects of market-oriented Cloud Computing are becoming now available and gaining popularity. In particular, some interesting initiatives show how different aspects of MOCC have been made available to wide public: fl exible pricing models, virtual market place, and market directories. (a) Flexible Pricing Models: Amazon Spot Instances.
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Amazon Web Services—one of the biggest players in the Infrastructure-as-a-Service market—has recently introduced the concept of spot instances 62
This is the content -  , which allows EC2 customers to bid on unused Amazon EC2 capacity and run those instances for as long as their bids exceed the current spot price. The spot price varies periodically according to the supply and the demand of EC2 instances, and is kept constant within a single hour block. Spot instances can be terminated at any time and they are usually priced at a lower price with respect to the traditional (on demand and reserved) instances, since they rely on the exceeding capacity available in the EC2 infrastructure. Therefore, it is the responsibility of the user to periodically persist the state of applications executing within spot instances. Spot instances represent an interesting opportunity for both Amazon and EC2 users to take benefi t from the current condition of the market: the provider can make revenue from a capacity that would have been wasted if priced at normal price, and the consumer can take the opportunity of paying less by taking major risks. Despite their volatile nature, spot instances have demonstrated to be reasonably reliable and usable for performing tasks that have a lower priority and are not critical. In other words, they are suitable for applications that can tolerate QoS limitations. Moreover, they are profi tably used to extend the capacity of an existing infrastructure at lower costs [130]. (b) Virtual Market Place: SpotCloud.
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  SpotCloud 63 is an online portal implementing a virtual market place, where sellers and buyers can reg-
This is the content -  ister and trade Cloud computing services. The platform is a market place operating in the Infrastructureas-a-Service sector. Buyers are looking for compute capacity that can meet the requirements of their applications, while sellers can make available their infrastructure to serve buyers' needs and make revenue from it. SpotCloud provides a comprehensive set of features that are expected for a virtual market place. Some of them include ● Detailed logging of all the buyers' transactions
- Full metering, billing for any capacity ● Full control over pricing and availability of capacity made available in the market
- Management of quotas and utilization levels for providers
- Federation management (many providers, many customers, but one platform)
- Hybrid Clouds support (internal and external resource management)
- Full market administration and reporting
- Applications and pre-build appliances directories
- Besides being an on-line portal, the virtual market realized by SpotCloud can also be replicated within
the private premises. Transactions are carried out with real money and based on credit that buyers and sellers have to top up once they create an account. 62 Full details about the spot instances service offering can be found in the Amazon Web Services at the following link: http://aws.
amazon.com/ec2/spot-instances/ [Retrieved: May 9, 2011].
63 http://www.spotCloud.com/
11.14
Mastering Cloud Computing
SpotCloud is the most representative implementation of a platform enabling market-oriented Cloud computing, even though with some limitations. The working principle of SpotCloud is a common and unique platform that sellers need to share in order to join the portal and make available computing capacity. SpotCloud currently supports Enomaly ECP 64 and OpenStack 65 . (c) Market Directories: AppSpot, The Cloud Market.
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  SpotCloud is implemented as an application hosted on AppSpot. This is a huge portal serving applications built on top of the Google AppEngine infrastructure: appspot.com is a namespace under which all the scal-
This is the content -  able Web applications developed with the Google AppEngine technology are made available to the community of Internet users. A solution that is more oriented towards listing available Cloud building blocks is The Cloud Market 66, which features a comprehensive listing of Amazon EC2 images. Even these solutions do not provide a complete implementation of a market directory. They constitute a step towards the realization of MOCC, since they provide means to easily locate components useful to build Cloud computing systems. 3. A Case Study: The Cloudbus Toolkit
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  An interesting case study coming from research is the Cloudbus toolkit [131]. This is a collection of tech-
This is the content -  nologies and components that comprehensively tries to address the challenges involved in realizing the vision of market-oriented Cloud Computing. Fig. 11.6 provides a comprehensive view of the cloudbus toolkit, and how they interact together to provide a platform for MOCC. Real-life applications belonging to different scenarios, such as fi nance, science, education, engineering, multimedia, and others, require Cloud computing facilities for processing their workloads and storing data. The Cloudbus toolkit can act as a general front-end by providing a mediated access to Cloud computing services that best serve the applications' needs. It does so by making available tools and technolo-
gies implementing a service brokering infrastructure and middleware for deploying applications in the Cloud. Brokering services are implemented by the Market maker, which allows users to take full advantage of the Cloud marketplace. It relies on different middleware implementations to fulfi ll the requests of users: these can be either Cloudbus technologies or third party implementations. Technologies such as Aneka or Workfl ow Engine provide services for executing applications in the Cloud. These can be public Clouds, private intranets, or data centers that can all be uniformly managed within an InterCloud [114] realm, which federates Computing Clouds belonging to different organizations into a unique domain characterization between agreements among parties. Table 11.1. Cloudbus Toolkit Components and Technologies.
Technology Description

http://www.enomaly.com/
http://www.openstack.org/

(Continued)

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  An interesting case study coming from research is the Cloudbus toolkit [131]. This is a collection of tech-
This is the content -  with a brief description of their function. The toolkit also includes facilities for developing algorithms and deployments in a simulated environment by means of CloudSim [132]. This is a toolkit enabling users to simulate many aspects of Cloud computing environments: from the basic building blocks of a Cloud (data centers, computing nodes, cores, network, and virtual machines) to the resource allocation algorithms and policies. As an example of its versatility, the toolkit has been used to simulate power aware scheduling policies [133] for reducing energy consumption when using large data-centers. physics optimization risk analysis 3D rendering blogging photos
biology modeling
banking imaging
<!-- image -->
teaching
IBM Blue Cloud
11.16
Mastering Cloud Computing
<!-- image -->
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  In this section, we have briefl y reviewed the fundamental concepts of market-oriented Cloud computing.
This is the content -  As for any technology, there exists a consolidation phase during which the concepts laying out its foun- dations are investigated, experimented, absorbed, and leveraged. Cloud computing is a relatively new evolution of distributed computing and has provided new ways to deliver IT infrastructure and services in a more effi cient way to both enterprise and single users. Initially, considerable development interest and effort have been oriented towards the consolidation of the Infrastructure-as-a-Service sector, and its integration within the existing computing systems and workfl ow processes. Currently, Platform-asa-Service solutions are gaining popularity and being used more consistently to deliver commercial and mainstream software applications and systems. This is the appropriate context where technologies and systems supporting market-oriented Cloud computing can be implemented and made available. Most of the work done in this sense still belongs to the domain of the academic research, even though the fi rst commercial and industrial implementations are reaching the wide audience. 11.3 FEDERATED CLOUDS/INTERCLOUD
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  In the previous section, we discussed how to defi ne models and systems for the trading of computing re-
This is the content -  sources as utilities, and how Cloud computing systems can interact with each other to make this trading happen. In this section, we address the same problem from the administrative and organizational point of view and introduce the concept of Cloud Federation and InterCloud. These are enablers for marketoriented Cloud computing since they provide means for interoperation among different Cloud providers. Cloud computing strongly implies the presence of fi nancial agreements between parties, since services are available on demand on a pay-per-use basis. Nonetheless, the concepts characterizing Cloud
Federation and InterCloud are applicable, with some limitations, for building aggregations of Clouds belonging to different administrative domains. 11.3.1 Characterization and Defi nition
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  The term "Cloud Federation " and "InterCloud " are often used interchangeably, and they convey the general meaning of an aggregation of Cloud computing providers, which have separate administrative do-
This is the content -  mains. It is important to clarify what these two terms mean and how do they apply to Cloud computing. The term "federation" implies to the creation of an organization that supersedes the decisional and administrative power of the single entities and acting as a whole 67 . Within a Cloud computing context, the word "federation" does not have such a strong connotation but implies that there are agreements between the different Cloud providers allowing them to leverage each others services in a privileged manner. A defi nition for Cloud Federation has been given by Reuven Cohen founder and CTO of Enomaly Inc. 68 : "Cloud federation manages consistency and access controls when two or more independent geo-
graphically distinct Clouds share either authentication, fi les, computing resources, command and control or access to storage resources." This defi nition is broad enough to include all the different expressions of aggregation of Cloud services that are governed by agreements between Cloud providers, rather than composed by the user. InterCloud is a term that is often used interchangeably to express the concept of Cloud federation. It has been introduced by Cisco for expressing a composition of Clouds that are interconnected by
means of open standards to provide a universal environment for levering Cloud computing services. By mimicking the Internet term, often referred as the "network of networks", InterCloud represents a "Cloud of Clouds"69 and therefore expresses the same concept of federating together Clouds belonging
67
This is the defi nition given by the Merriam Webster online dictionary: http://www.merriam-Webster.com/dictionary/federation
[Retrieved: May 10, 2011].
68 http://www.elasticvapor.com/2008/08/standardized-Cloud.html [Retrieved: May 10, 2011]. 69 http://samj.net/2009/06/interCloud-is-global-Cloud-of-Clouds.html
Advanced Topics in Cloud Computing
11.17
to different administrative organizations. Whereas this is in many cases acceptable, some practitioners
70
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  The term "Cloud Federation " and "InterCloud " are often used interchangeably, and they convey the general meaning of an aggregation of Cloud computing providers, which have separate administrative do-
This is the content -  and experts—like Ellen Rubin, founder and VP of Products at CloudSwitch —prefer to give different connotation to the two terms: "The primary difference between the InterCloud and federation is that the InterCloud is based on fu- ture standards and open interfaces, while federation uses a vendor version of the control plane. With the InterCloud vision, all Clouds will have a common understanding of how applications should be deployed. Eventually workloads submitted to a Cloud will include enough of a defi nition (resources, security, service level, geo-location, etc.) that the Cloud is able to process the request and deploy the application. This will create the true utility model, where all the requirements are met by the defi nition and the application can execute "as is" in any Cloud with the resources to support it." Therefore, the term "InterCloud" refers mostly to a global vision where interoperability among different
Cloud providers is governed by standards, thus creating an open platform where applications can shift workloads and freely compose services from different sources. On the contrary, the concept of Cloud Federation is more general and also includes ad hoc aggregations between Cloud providers on the basis of private agreements and proprietary interfaces. 11.3.2 Cloud Federation Stack
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  Cloud Federation involves research and development at different levels: conceptual, logical and op-
This is the content -  erational, and infrastructural. Figure 11.7 provides a comprehensive view of the challenges faced to design and implement an organizational structure that coordinates together Cloud services belonging to different administrative domains and makes them operate within a context of a single unifi ed service middleware.
Conceptual Level
<!-- image -->
Motivations
Each level presents different challenges and operates at a different layer of the IT stack. It then re- quires the use of different approach and technologies. Taken together, the solutions to the challenges faced at each of these levels constitute a reference model for Cloud Federation.
70
CloudSwitch is a Cloud company whose focus is on delivering an enterprise gateway to several Cloud computing systems. http://
www.Cloudswitch.com/page/Cloud-federation-and-the-interCloud [Retrieved: May 10, 2011].
11.18
Mastering Cloud Computing
<!-- image -->
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  The conceptual level addresses the challenges in presenting Cloud Federation as a favorable solution
This is the content -  with respect to the use of services leased by single Cloud providers. In this level, it is important to clearly identify the advantages of either service providers or service consumers in joining a federation and delineate the new opportunities that a federated environment creates with respect to the single provider solution. Elements of concern at this level are ● Motivations for Cloud providers to join a federation
- Motivations for service consumer to leverage a federation ● Advantages for providers in leasing their services to other providers
- Obligations of providers once they have joined the federation
- Trust agreements between providers
- Transparency versus consumers
- Among the aspects listed above, the most relevant ones are the motivations of both service providers
and consumers in joining a federation. From the perspective of Cloud service providers, being part of a federation is favorable if it helps increasing their revenue and if it provides new opportunities to increase their business. Moreover, the option of joining a federation can also be considered convenient if it helps sustaining the quality of service ensured to customers in case of peak-loads, which put extreme demand on the infrastructure of the single provider. More precisely, it is possible to identify functional and non-functional requirements that Cloud service providers have behind these motivations. The functional requirements include the following: ● Supplying low-latency access to customers regardless their location. It is very unlikely that single
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  The conceptual level addresses the challenges in presenting Cloud Federation as a favorable solution
This is the content -  - Cloud providers have a capillary distribution of their datacenters. Therefore, services that require low-latency might provide poor performance because of unfortunate geo-location. Within this scenario, the federation might help the single providers to deliver the same service and to meet the expected QoS. ● Handling bursts in demand. Even though Cloud computing gives the illusion of infi nite capacity and continuous availability, service providers rely on a fi nite IT infrastructure that will be eventu-
- ally fully utilized. A natural solution to this problem is increasing the infrastructure by adding more capacity: in order to keep up with the increasing demand for storage and computation, Google has increased its number of servers from 8000 to more than 450000 in 5 years and moved from 4 server farms to more than 60 data centers; while Facebook has recently doubled its datacenter capacity. Such huge provisions are affordable for large IT companies that can make appropriate forecast about an increasing demand. Irregular demand can be better addressed by renting capacity from other providers, since not every Cloud provider is in the position of being an IT giant. Cloud federation facilitates such activity, by providing a context within the lease of resources or services. ● Scaling existing applications and services beyond the capabilities of the owned infrastructure. The need of additional capacity can also originate from the growth in scale of existing applica-
- tions which are temporarily hosted and do not constitute a vital part of the service provider's core business. Again the opportunities of leasing additional services from a federated provider can constitute a potential advantage of Cloud Federation. ● Make revenue from unused capacity. In order to provide the illusion of continuous availability and infi nite capacity, Cloud service providers generally own large computing systems, which gener-
- ate costs in terms of maintenance and power consumption despite their real use. Energy effi cient computing solutions can help in reducing the costs and the impact of IT over the environment. A different opportunity is given by the Cloud Federation where providers can lease their services to other providers for a limited period of time and thus making revenue even without direct customers.
Advanced Topics in Cloud Computing
11.19
The motivations for joining a Cloud Federation also include non-functional requirements. The most relevant are the following: ● Meeting compulsory regulations about the location of data. Geo-location might become an is-
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  The conceptual level addresses the challenges in presenting Cloud Federation as a favorable solution
This is the content -  - sue limiting the capability of a provider in serving consumers. In this particular scenario, lack of capacity on the provider side is not the reason for leveraging the federation, but the opportunity for identifying a provider that is in a position of delivering the service to the customer because of the location of its datacenter. Geo-location of data becomes an important matter in case Cloud services deal with confi dential data that requires specifi c levels of secrecy. Different countries have different regulations with respect to—for instance—the level of access that government institutions may have on confi dential data. ● Containing transient spikes in operational costs. Operational costs can experience temporary spikes when there is a sudden change in the power of electricity due to natural disasters. This
- situation makes it inconvenient to fully exploit a given datacenter and provides an opportunity for leveraging federation resources to deliver services at a cheaper price. ● Disaster recovery. Natural disasters happen and they can put an entire data center or more out of service for an undefi ned period of time, if they are co-located. In this scenario, agreements
- between different providers to handle disaster condition are more likely to be settled within a federated context compared to a competitive market. For all the above cases, Cloud Federation not only helps in providing conceptual solutions but also
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  The conceptual level addresses the challenges in presenting Cloud Federation as a favorable solution
This is the content -  practical means to realize these goals. Cloud Federation is an overlay that mostly benefi ts Cloud service providers and that is supposed to be transparent to service consumers. Besides the indirect benefi ts that end users get, there are indeed some potential direct benefi ts originating specifi cally from the concept of federation. Indirect benefi ts are mostly related to the quality of service perceived by the end users. Real quality of service is possible by enforcing admission control, which ensures that if a request is accepted, it will be served in compliance with the QoS profi le defi ned in the SLA signed with the customer. Currently, the major Cloud service providers engage QoS agreements, which are mostly based on availability rather than other quality factors. For instance, in an IaaS scenario, the published hardware features of a VM instance might not mirror its real performance. Since there is no SLA enforcement on such features, the provider will always try to serve requests even by risking delivery of a poor performance. Within a federated scenario, requests may be served by leveraging other providers, thus ensuring that the expected performance profi le is met. Therefore, as indirect benefi t for the users, Cloud Federation can help to increase the overall QoS experienced by the user when requesting for a service. Direct benefi ts instead constitute something that advantage end users and they are perceivable because of the existence of federated Clouds. Cloud providers offering different services can support each other since they are not competitors. A good example can be taken from the cooperation between the airline and accommodation market seg-
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  The conceptual level addresses the challenges in presenting Cloud Federation as a favorable solution
This is the content -  ments. Airline companies provide you with selected options for accommodation to be paired with a fl ight booking. This is generally the result of an agreement between the companies that might support each other, thus providing a better service to the customer. Since companies operating in the two sectors are not competing with each other, they can gain advantage if they provide customers with a complete solution. It is possible to replicate this type of collaboration in federated Cloud computing environment. For instance, providers that reside in different market segments (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) might advertise each other to provide a better service to the user. Enterprises that have legacy systems will be primarily looking at IaaS solutions to deploy and scale their systems. IaaS vendors can complement their offer with advantageous access to some PaaS services by selecting those that might be complementary, of interest to the user, and offered by federated providers. In the future, Amazon AWS might provide discounted access to AppEngine or simply provide a better interaction with the services exposed by Google in terms of data transfer, network connection, and bandwidth. How does this help the customer? The same company that is already hosting its Web application on Amazon EC2 might want to integrate in the future new features and develop them with a scalable technology. Due to performance advantages gained in leveraging AppEngine from an EC2
11.20
Mastering Cloud Computing deployment, this could be the solutions of choice. This is more likely to be possible within the context
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  The conceptual level addresses the challenges in presenting Cloud Federation as a favorable solution
This is the content -  of a Cloud Federation. Moreover, federated Clouds can provide a better service to users, even if they reside in the same market segment but provide different services. For instance, within an IaaS scenario, a specifi c provider might not be able to serve VM templates for hosting a specifi c operating system, but can suggest or point the customer to another provider able to supply that capability. This is applicable if the two providers have mutual agreements, which are facilitated by belonging to a federation. Being part of the federation also implies obligations of providers in order to avoid parasitic behaviors. For instance, each provider is expected to be an active member of the federation by contributing with its resources. This makes an organization such as the federation dependable and increases the trust that each provider puts on it. Obligations, such as making always available a fraction of resources and services to the federation, might be considered as disadvantages but they may also constitute potential benefi ts. For instance, large companies such as Google are charged for energy usage according to the peak request rather than detailed used usage over a month [135]. This means that if in one month, a datacenter reaches 90% of peak capacity and on an average works at 60%, it will pay power bills for the cost of operating at 90% capacity for the entire month. This has led companies to put a lot of effort in optimizing the utilization of a data center. Cloud federation might be an alternative to frenetic optimization since they might make available internal resource for usage by other members of the federation. The revenue obtained from leasing these resources is an opportunity to compensate the energy costs for peak request. All these aspects provide a rationale for the existence of a federation of Clouds. Obstacles at the conceptual level are constituted by the implications on security and trust. For instance, within a feder-
ated context, a provider might offl oad a portion of the service consumers' requests to another provider it has agreements with. This is done transparently to the user, who might not desire such behavior. These are challenges that have to be properly addressed in order to make the concept of Cloud Federation a viable way to effi ciently exploit Cloud computing as a technology. 2. Logical and Operational Level
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  The logical and operational level identifi es and addresses the challenges in devising a framework enabling
This is the content -  the aggregation of providers belonging to different administrative domains within a context of a single overlay infrastructure, which is the Cloud Federation. At this level, policies and rules for interoperation are defi ned. Moreover, this is the layer where decisions on how and when to lease a service to—or to leverage a service from—another provider are taken. The logical component defi nes a context within which agreements among different providers are settled and services are negotiated, while the operational component characterizes and shapes the dynamic behavior of the federation as a result of the choices of the single providers. This is the level where market oriented Cloud computing is implemented and realized. It is important at this level, to address the following challenges:
● How to represent a federation?
●
How to model and represent a Cloud service, a Cloud provider, or an agreement?
- How to defi ne the rules and policies that allow providers to join a federation? ● What are the mechanisms in place for settling agreements among providers?
- What are the responsibilities that providers have with respect to each other?
- When to take advantage of the federation?
- Which kinds of services are more likely to be leased or bought?
- How to price resources that are leased and which fraction of resources to lease?
- The logical and operational level provides opportunities for both the academia and the industry. Where-
as the need of a federation—or more generally some sort of interoperation—has now been assessed, there is no common and clear guideline for defi ning a model for Cloud Federation and addressing the challenges listed above. Indeed, several initiatives are developing. On the research side, several research works are investigating organizational models for Cloud interoperability and studying economical models for describing the behavior of Cloud service providers and service consumers in a federated
Advanced Topics in Cloud Computing
11.21
environment. Within the industry, the major IT giants are working on promoting standards and drafting proposals for interoperable Clouds. A particular attention in this level has been put on the necessity of Service Level Agreements and their defi nition [150]. The need for SLAs is an accepted fact in both academy and industry since they
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  The logical and operational level identifi es and addresses the challenges in devising a framework enabling
This is the content -  defi ne more clearly what is leased or bought between different providers. Moreover, they allow assessing whether the services traded are delivered according to the expected quality profi le. It is, then, possible to specify policies that regulate the transactions among providers and establish penalties in case of degraded service delivery. This is particularly important, because it increases the level of trust that each party puts in Cloud Federation. Service Level Agreements are in use since 1980 and originated in the telecommunication domain [152] in order to defi ne the Quality of Service attached to a contract between a consumer and a network provider. From there on, they have been used in several fi elds including Web Services, Grid computing, and Cloud computing. The specifi c nature of SLA varies from domain to domain, but it can be generally defi ned as "an explicit statement of expectations and obligations that exist in a business relationship between two organizations: the service provider and the service consumer". SLAs defi ne the performance delivery ability of the provider, the consumers' performance profi le, and means to monitor and measure the delivered performance. An implementation of a SLA should specify [153]: ● Purpose. Objectives to achieve by using a SLA.
●
Restrictions. Necessary steps or actions that need to be taken to ensure that the requested level
- of service is delivered.
- Validity period. Period of time during which the SLA is valid. ● Scope. Services that will be delivered to the consumer and services that are out of the SLA.
- Parties. Any involved organizations or individual and their roles (e.g., provider and consumer).
- Service Level Objectives (SLOs). Levels of services which both parties agree on. These are ex-
- pressed by means of service level indicators such as availability, performance, and reliability.
- Penalties. The penalties that will occur if the delivered service does not achieve the defi ned SLOs.
- Optional services. Services that are not mandatory but might be required. ● Administration. Processes that are used to guarantee that SLOs are achieved and the related
- organization's responsibilities for controlling these processes.
- Parties that are willing to operate under a service level agreement engage in a multi-step process that
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  The logical and operational level identifi es and addresses the challenges in devising a framework enabling
This is the content -  generally involves the discovery or creation of the SLA, its operational phase, and its termination once its validity is over or there has been a violation of the contract [154]. A more articulated process has been detailed by the Sun Internet Data Center Group, which includes six steps for the SLA life cycle: ● Discover service provide .
●
Defi ne SLA
- Establish agreement
- ●
Monitor SLA violation
- Terminate SLA
- Enforce penalties for SLA violation
- Currently, a very rudimental level of SLA management is present and the interoperability among differ-
ent Cloud providers is mostly characterized by ad-hoc aggregation. The proposed process constitutes a reference model that still needs to be realized rather than a common implementation. Considerable work has been done in the academic domain regarding each of the steps defi ned before [150], but within industry, SLAs are still unilateral arrangements that are imposed by service providers and that the user can only accept rather than negotiate. 3. Infrastructural Level
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  The infrastructural level addresses the technical challenges involved in enabling heterogeneous Cloud
This is the content -  computing systems to interoperate seamlessly. It deals with the technology barriers that keep separate
11.22
Mastering Cloud Computing
<!-- image -->
Cloud computing systems belonging to different administrative domains. By having standardized pro- tocols and interfaces, these barriers can be overcome. In other words, this level for the federation is
what the TCP/IP stack is for the Internet: a model and a reference implementation of the technologies enabling the interoperation of systems. The infrastructural level lays its foundations in the IaaS and PaaS layers of the Cloud computing reference model (discussed in Section 4.2). Services for interoperation and interface may also fi nd implementation at the SaaS level, especially for the realization of negotiations and of federated Clouds. At this level, it is important to address the following issues: ● What kind of standards to use?
●
How to design interfaces and protocols for interoperation?
- Which are the technologies to use for interoperation? ● How to realize a software system, design platform components, and services enabling interoper-
- ability?
- Interoperation and composition among different Cloud computing vendors is possible only by means of
---------chunk ended--------------

---------chunk started--------------
This the heading -  The infrastructural level addresses the technical challenges involved in enabling heterogeneous Cloud
This is the content -  open standards and interfaces. Moreover, interfaces and protocols change considerably at each layer of the Cloud computing reference model. Being the more mature, the IaaS layer is the one that has evolved more in this sense. Almost every IaaS provider exposes Web interfaces for packaging virtual machine templates, launching, monitoring and terminating virtual instances. Even though not standardized, these interfaces leverage the Web services and are quite similar to each other. The use of a common technology simplifi es the interoperation among vendors since a minimum amount of code is required to enable such interoperation. These APIs allow for a defi ning an abstraction layer that can uniformly access the services of several IaaS vendors. There already exist tools—both open source and commercial—and specifi cations that provide interoperability by implementing such layer. In order to fully support the vision of a Cloud Federation, more sophisticated capabilities need to be implemented. For instance, the possibility of dynamically moving virtual machine instances among different providers is essential to support dynamic load balancing among different IaaS vendors. In this direction, the Open Virtualization Format (OVF) [51] aims to be a solution for this problem, which may eventually be successful, since it has been endorsed by several Cloud computing vendors.
